item.title: Re: This the kind of thing that is killing the site
item.content:encoded: This article was posted two years ago, and somehow the site has kept on thriving ever since. Any claims that "this __ is killing the site" is laughable, and outdated.

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Moderator Post
item.content:encoded:
the problem is that the supposed "subjugation" technique by the AI is to post pictures of a baked image with the word 'tendies' plastered on it, it creates nothing but mood whiplash when the rest of the article is trying to be serious

This is a valid criticism of the piece and is a reasonable opinion to have. Despite that, it is betrayed by your previous arguments. Going back to your original comment:

it serves as the author's personal revenge-rant on alt-righters strawmen over a controversy that shouldn't have happened in the first place on both sides, that aside, this didn't even try to do it gracefully

That is a tacit defense of people like ghost. You even specifically use the term "alt-right" before calling the depiction a straw man. As I said, I've no problem whatsoever with good-faith critique, but excusing and defending the alt-right is not a thing done in good faith, so it calls your entire critique into question.

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Moderator Post
item.content:encoded:
Of course there are people who believe in that crap, that is what humans do, the problem is that the supposed "subjugation" technique by the AI is to post pictures of a baked image with the word 'tendies' plastered on it, it creates nothing but mood whiplash when the rest of the article is trying to be serious, seeing as how the whole message is about the transformative power of memes on our society, wouldn't have it made sense to start with the "aggressive memes"? It would start more flame wars, have people gnawing at each other on Social Media, etc…

People aren't going to start an argument over 'tendies', they maybe would in the top image, they would start arguments over things that take such a drastic side that it becomes a point of derision.

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Moderator Post
item.content:encoded:
You can dislike the article, that's fine. How you express that dislike is where we run into issues.

Checking dates, this does seem to be posted about when the pride debacle happened, but that seems a bit less relevant to this piece given that it was posted once before and had existed as a draft for quite a while before posting. I think it's fair to chalk that all up to poor timing.

As far as the "Kafka trap" goes, it may not exactly be the most graceful of replies, but there is an argument to be made that a defense of the alt-right (a group whose name was coined by a literal neo-Nazi, by the way) in the middle of the pride flag crisis can safely be assumed as not being made in good faith. The things that the ghost in this article believes are objectively disgusting and they mirror the manifestos of real world killers such as Elliot Rogers. Some in this comments section, including yourself, have called the character a straw man, but given that these kinds of people do actually exist in the world, I'm not inclined to consider that a valid criticism. Defending people like this or handwaving their existence away as a soapbox fantasy is… well, bad. They do exist and they do hurt people. Defending them implies tolerance of those beliefs, which is not a good look.

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Moderator Post
item.content:encoded:
I am sorry for arguing in bad faith, I am just tired and that messed with my ability to not go off the rails, thanks for catching that.

But my opinion still stands, I would also like to point out that just because I have only become a user recently doesn't mean that I haven't lurked on this website for years.

Also, wasn't this posted around the time the whole "Pride Flag Controversy" happened?

"If the character in this article is the kind of person that you can relate to, the problem isn't the article my dude."

Additionally, I do think it is very un-professional to attempt to put someone in a kafka trap for giving criticism, I do hope that this is not a common occurence among higher level staff, or that indicates that something truly is killing the site.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Moderator Post
item.content:encoded:
PaniniMan, this comment is a flagrant violation of the Criticism Policy, as well as Rule Zero.

+ A big 'ol list of What Not To Do when giving critique
- A big 'ol list of What Not To Do when giving critique

This the kind of thing that is killing the site

Probably not a great idea to assert about what is "killing the site" given you've been here for literally six days. The site isn't anywhere close to dying and authors writing what they want to write is not "killing" it.

This SCP does not fit that purpose

It doesn't have to fit any "purpose" outside of entertaining enough readers to sit above a net rating of -10.

author's personal revenge-rant on alt-righters strawmen over a controversy that shouldn't have happened in the first place on both sides

Not entirely sure what specific controversy you're discussing, but the article deals with extreme far right memes. Not so sure how those are anyone's fault except for the people who create and/or propagate them?

*Explains the story because the article itself was too poorly written to provide adequate information*

This is a bad faith argument. The article is competently written and tells a cohesive narrative, even if much of the story and context is implied. Not everybody will catch every detail, and that's fine—preferable, even.

I mean, come on, the whole AI's schtick is to somehow turn people into social recluses by showing them a baked image with the word TENDIES, what next, the picture of the funny dog eating a pepsi is capable of creating an EK class End of Consciousness scenario?

Any argument decrying absurdity on this website is hilariously tone-deaf. You do realize what site you are on, right? There is an SCP that is literally a nuclear-powered dildo that vibrates fast enough to make things explode.

Literally just take out most of the sections and the plot remains the same, once again pointing to the fact that this is not meant to entertain and instead is to serve as a revenge rant

I could remove basically all of Jules Winfield's lines from Pulp Fiction and the plot would basically remain unchanged. Does that mean Tarantino fucked up his story by including pointless dialogue? Hint: No, it doesn't. Strong characterization is more interesting than weak characterization. The characterization of ghost is integral to the central themes of this article—a fact that you both admit and write off as pointless in the same sentence.

this goes for both sides

I don't know why you keep saying this? It has no bearing on the article.

Last time I checked the word 'cuck' and 'cuckold' began circulating around 2014-early 2015

"Cuckold" has been a word since before Modern English was a language and the word "cuck" has existed as slang on the internet since at least 2007 if Urban Dictionary's definition history is to be believed.

Really? A self-insert, wow, totally does not further the point that you have become too involved in making SCPs and are now resorting to using this website as a basis for your soapbox, which should be viewed as nothing more than a delete-able offense for articles.

Good thing you can't delete articles then, innit? Self-inserts have been a part of the wiki since the beginning, and pretending that they're now uncouth all of a sudden is laughable. I again point to the fact that you have been here for 6 days.

TL;DR: Most of your criticisms are stemming from a misplaced understanding of what the SCP Wiki is and what we allow. Namely, we allow people to post just about whatever they want to as long as it gets more upvotes than downvotes and doesn't break our content rules. If you plan to critique works here in the future, I suggest arguing in good faith.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Mod Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
PaniniMan, the post you are replying to is nearly two years old. This is considered necroposting. Don't do this.

This is a CLOSED staff post. Do not reply unless you are staff.

item.title: This the kind of thing that is killing the site
item.content:encoded:
Let me start by first and foremost saying that I have been lurking for years, and let me tell you, it is not fun looking back to now and seeing how things have devolved so badly, so let me get this rant:

+ show block
– hide block

The main purpose of this article

Let me say that, even though I did go through the years of seeing Tales that now belong in the LolFoundation Canon, at least they served a purpose that was shared by other Tales and SCPs, and that was to entertain (Whether by creating scares, being funny, heartwarming, etc…) and to show a story in a contained format.

This SCP does not fit that purpose, instead it serves as the author's personal revenge-rant on alt-righters strawmen over a controversy that shouldn't have happened in the first place on both sides, that aside, this didn't even try to do it gracefully, it instead feels overtly stagnated and the overall absurdity and outright seething from the author spilling into the article skyrockets with every. single. section, and, in an effort to say "HAHA TAKE THIS", the author instead shows their own immaturity and types in a frenzied tantrum.

The author's response to criticism

When met with criticism and/or general questions, the author has shown to

  • Kafka trap the person criticizing
  • "I was intending to be rude and outright dickish"
  • *Explains the story because the article itself was too poorly written to provide adequate information*

Absurdity

I mean, come on, the whole AI's schtick is to somehow turn people into social recluses by showing them a baked image with the word TENDIES, what next, the picture of the funny dog eating a pepsi is capable of creating an EK class End of Consciousness scenario? Please… just stop.

Plot problems

Literally just take out most of the sections and the plot remains the same, once again pointing to the fact that this is not meant to entertain and instead is to serve as a revenge rant, I mean, seriously, what DOES the sections about the dude talking about how much he hates the non-whites and bought a gun actually do for the story? Nothing! There is no reason whatsoever that these should be in the article by writing standards, they are un-needed, the focus is supposed to be on the SCP, not the dude's stupid rants. Anybody who tries to weaponize jokes as propaganda and means of achieving accelerationism, not to actually create a release of dopamine, deserves to be mocked extensively for it, this goes for both sides.

Continuity Issues

Last time I checked the word 'cuck' and 'cuckold' began circulating around 2014-early 2015, additionally, the bizarre humor found today in internet memes was not around then back in 2010-2011, why do you think there is a distinction between the two, yet again proving that this is nothing but one long seethe from the author.

Character Issues

The characters suck in general, regardless of the fact that they really don't need to be included.

- "From the Office of Director Jean Karlyle Aktus"

Really? A self-insert, wow, totally does not further the point that you have become too involved in making SCPs and are now resorting to using this website as a basis for your soapbox, which should be viewed as nothing more than a delete-able offense for articles.

TLDR;
"You certainly have a flair for the dramatic."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is an entirely different thing to outright Kafka trap someone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda want to see this this thing interacting with the GAW. It'd be a meme war that would probably end with Jude fighting 3625 in some kind of computer world. Heh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I really get this but it's… interesting? Reading through the "ghost"'s blogs genuinely disgusted me, as I'm aware that many people genuinely think down this line of thought. For a time, even I did, although to a much lesser extent. If I had to take a guess at what your goal with this was, I'd say it was to make fun of people who think that memes are either destroying society (boomers I guess?) and those who think they're very important and use them for… I dunno, nazi shit? I'm finding it hard to get any clear meaning out of this but it's definitely an interesting read. tentative +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of memes becoming complete nonsense over time and corrupting human behavior by creating a false sense of community is great, but the political implications are obnoxious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is an alt-right meme machine scarier than most things i've seen on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've made a promise that i'll burn every Kekistan shirt I see.
So far I'm at zero burns because i'm fucking lazy BUT HEY i'm doing my best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing more evocative than the article is the fact that it only has +81 at time of posting.

In all honesty, though, what really fascinates me is how enthralling Rose's monologue is. The language is intentionally and effectively grandiose, and it's worrying that I wasn't so disgusted by it. Similar to how the beginning of The Way It Ends made me forget, for a second, that we were talking about murder.

There will be many who think they are in on the joke, and will repeat these memes ironically, ignorant that they are at the climax of the performance. They are the punchline. They are so easy to manipulate - they long so desperately to be right. To be strong. To make up for where they are lacking.

Had I not been forcibly red-pilled to the Left by my trans ass, this might have been me. Now I'm having awkward memories of my old friend group buying Kekistan t-shirts. I still haven't thrown it out.

Imma go do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus Christ, this was horrifying. Yet another SCP that was so viscerally disturbing to me that I felt like I had to lie down and take a nap after reading just to recover from it. I really can't think of what else to say: this was sick and twisted and awful and scared me in ways that almost no other article on this site has managed. I almost feel bad for upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It also breaks typical SCP style writing

No it doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get it I think, It's posting an absurd amount of memes but what makes it so dangerous? wouldn't posting constant memes just make it akin to a spam bot? Maybe some of the comments here came from before the edits that were mentioned.

It also breaks typical SCP style writing just to be a "take that" against the alt right, like that one bit about the guy being impotent.

Gonna have to be a down vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All horror is political; the best stuff, imo, acknowledges this and actually seeks to say something other than "status quo (read: hegemony) is god".

+1, and I'd do it again if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally got around to reading this, and fuck was it sickening.

I just felt empty reading the ghost’s notes, because I know there’s almost nothing exaggerated about them. There are thousands of people who are just like that, and the internet has given them a voice and a platform to spread their toxicity.

+1 for dressing up a documentary script as an SCP, you mad genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The page for the Pepe the Frog image on wikimedia commons was deleted with the reason of "(Derivative work of non-free content (F3): derivative of Matt Furie's pepe the frog)". Something tells me the page was incorrectly created and isn't compliant with our license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ghost just doesn't feel right to me. Not that I don't buy that people like the ghost exist, they clearly do, but rather that the ghost we're told about by Rose seems to be a far more competent and put-together individual than the one we see in their own words.

+ Show Block
- Close

The person in their own words is someone who's jacking off on pictures of liberal politicians and thinking that their writing will make them the next messiah. But the story requires that someone in that mental state be nonetheless able to maintain a relationship over a fairly long term, with someone who they've got so convinced of their own rightness that they'd literally die for them. Yes, it's an ABUSIVE relationship, but abusers are typically pretty skilled at manipulation. This person is also meant to be someone who's willing to put together a plan that could take well over a decade to finally succeed. Despite it being a plan that he seems to not quite understand what it will be (in that he seems to think he'll be doing something like restructuring the world, whereas the final plan would be one of propaganda and long-term manipulation.)

M reads like Elliot Rodger, but that guy's plan for his grand striking back at society was to drive around in his car and shoot people, and upload some stuff to youtube. Whereas M's plan was to seduce a brilliant computer programmer, brainwash her into his ideology, convince her to create a program that would calculate the best method for restructuring the world to his whims, and then set it in motion.

If the writing had given more character to Diana, I might be able to fill in this missing peace. It is a little uncomfortable in that this is a story where a major figure in it is a woman in an abusive relationship, and yet she's the one who's the least characterized. Her abuser is given several paragraphs to tell us who he is and why he does what he does, while she feels like a plot device.

For example, it's entirely possible that, rather than this being entirely his plan, she's as much interested in restructuring the world into a conservative 'utopia' as he is. That would bridge the gap, in that I could see something that would motivate her to stay with him. He's helping provide the resources for the plan, and they share an interest in taking over the world and making it a right-wing utopia.

Or perhaps they'd been in a relationship before he descended into total right-wing whackjobbery, and as a result, and thus she's putting up with and going through with this because she hopes that once she finishes this project, she could get him back the way he was.

If Rose or Diana or whoever else was involved in removing this guy's name from the world, then why not have them have gone farther, remove all his writing, and tell the story more from Diana's perspective? That would make him feel more consistent, and give more voice to a character who feels like she should be the linchpin of the whole story.

Alternately, cutting Diana out entirely would work too, because it would make it seem more plausible that Rose is a much more anomalous thing. Mad scientists are a pretty common idea, and there's always sort of a feeling that the results of their mad science are as much creating themselves as they are doing the work. It would also make more sense that he goes for the 'Run over and kill cops' option, because it no longer requires as much long-term planning. He makes a computer in a fit of mad-science brilliance, and then is disappointed that its result would take well over a decade. Since that wouldn't require him having the patience for seduction+manipulation+someone else doing his work, that could easily have him go with a 'Plan B.'

Though I suppose my last problem, and I rather suspect this one is a result of my not actually comprehending the article, but how exactly is Rose an anomaly? She's a computer program. An extremely well-made one, but a program nonetheless, and it seems to imply that Rose isn't somehow anomalously bypassing their protections, she's simply able to upload from within the cage because she has backups. As far as I can tell, Rose is entirely within the reasonable bounds of known science, so I'm not sure why the Foundation would go out of their way to contain her. The Foundation doesn't seem like the type to contain non-anomalies, even potentially apocalyptic ones. They didn't stop the atom bomb, they didn't step in and solve the Cuban Missile Crisis. They've sat by during genocides and murders. Why step in on Rose?

I guess aside from those issues, the real part of the problem is that the article makes the threat seem a lot less scary. One way or another, the simple fact that Rose is contained means that this whole threat that would exist in the real world is not that big a deal. The Foundation is attempting to contain Rose. That means the Foundation is willing to act to stop Rose from going through with her plans. So what if the alt-right takes power? It gained power through means the Foundation was willing to attempt to contain. The Foundation can deal with all manner of threats that normal people can barely comprehend. I'm pretty sure they can rig an election. The only thing stopping them from doing it, in this world, would appear to be that they can't actually be sure that Rose's plan is really working. But if we reach a point where there can be massive civilian casualties (i.e. The alt-right controlling all relevant government organs so that LGBT people and immigrants etc will be humiliated and tortured and destroyed) then I can't imagine the Foundation saying "Well, I dunno, this COULD be non-anomalous."

I really want to like this. It may be a bit heavy-handed, but that's not necessarily a bad thing. But as it is, it just doesn't connect. The horror would apply if I plausibly believed a thing like Rose could exist, but if Rose is in Foundation custody, then the Foundation will step in. But the fact that I don't find it that horrifying isn't really important, and it's not like you're writing to me personally, clearly many people do. My bigger problem is that it's a story with a confusing antagonist, where the key figure is a domestic abuse victim who barely feels present, while her abuser is clearly defined (even if I feel like his definition goes against what he's portrayed as having accomplished.) I feel like Diana should be better characterized so I can understand who she is and what she's going through, or else removed entirely. Personally, I'd prefer the former, since it would have the additional effect of me no longer thinking "How the fuck was this man able to maintain even a dysfunctional relationship?"

Please collapse long posts ~ Leveritas

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a lot to like about this - solidly written, thought-provoking (in multiple senses) and lingering.

The only thing that has stopped me upvoting already is that I struggle to get a handle on Diana as a character. What was her motivation here? I find it quite difficult to see her influence clearly in the character of Rose, as M's views are so much more clearly identifiable. Diana is de-personified by M, but the article doesn't give her a clear personality (at least for me). I'm not sure whether it's ironic that, given its punchline, the article as written gives M far more of a voice (and thus a "legacy") than Diana herself.

I had hoped to hear more about this in the comments, and would be interested in views.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
most of the dont act like cartoon villains

Wouldn't it not be a stretch to say that a few might? Or, in this case, at least one?

although i dont doubt there are at least a few who truly think like that

Oh. So it's a reasonable premise, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, lots are almost 100% edgelords, but a reasonable number are relatively sane (at least…in my experience) but their political views are fucked up for countless reasons, only the truly abhorrent (who are probably >20% of the movement) take on the beliefs because of being deeply hateful.

basically, my point is that most of the dont act like cartoon villains 24/7 even if their actual views line up pretty well with that shit. So for me it makes the characters hard to believe, although i dont doubt there are at least a few who truly think like that, but they dont really constitute the existentially threatening aspect of the group.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see this sentiment that the neo-natzi ghost was too unbelievable echoed a lot and let me just say: most of them are like this.

They're a bunch of 2edgy misanthropes losers who hype up memes as the power to topple nations. Not the most prominent ones, of course, or else they wouldn't have built up as big a movement as they did. But the rank and file nerd reich morons, hell, even the big fish like Weev and Anglin, act almost exactly like the ghost does here. Maybe they're less dramatic (or maybe not), but there really is less to the modern neo-natzi than they'll tell you there is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly i like the concept and the imagery a lot.

Unfortunately, its bogged down by trying too hard to appear grand and destructive. Now clearly, the article voices an opinion that memes are more destructive than we give them credit for. But it is exaggerated to a point the really breaks my suspension of disbelief.

In a way it reminds me of the Russian sleep experiment, where the author tries to make it this d33p commentary with all these edgy pseudo-philosophical remarks.

Nearly everything ive said has been echoed a million times so its redundant at this point.

The last thing i have to say is that the political commentary is'nt necessarily bad but like everyone else said, the characters are essentially acting as a comical representation of a member of the alt right. The result is a take on a political issue that fails to raise any legitimately thought provoking arguments against them.

Personally i would have liked it more if you showed the seductive side of the alt right, the way they build these cults of personality, appeal to peoples masculinity, and the way that many members hype themselves up as masters of philosophy, debate, rationalism, etc. I say this is someone who was borderline alt-right when they were younger. Obviously this idea would require an almost toally different article, but it could have provided a terrifying look into the way we fall under the sway of ideology, which is done here in a way that really lack nuance in my view.

so yeah, its a downvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But her personality, which is totally independent of those systems, is both working towards creating a situation that directly benefits the ideologies of the ghost, while at the same time acknowledging the idiocy of his intentions and mocking his motivations, even while she's bound by them.

AKA, The actual thoughts of 90% of internet users, hence why this is scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow! This is fucking terrifying in a way few skips have accomplished!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I adore this.

I was unsettled, if only because it reminded me of actual spree killers, specifically in the diary entries. Those were uncomfortably similar that it wouldn't have surprised me if it was taken directly, word for word from one.

It's unapologetic in what it is, and I love it for that.

+1 good work kaktus

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta tell you, this article is so meta I can't even.

So, the article itself is a jab at the alt right (and possibly RPC) and, specifically, two of it'a favourite pastimes:

1) making mind-numbingly philistine readings of art that, despite all their purple prose, fails to grasp the most basic arguments of the piece.

2) whining incessently about the decadence of modern culture, insisting that people who don't agree with them are too stupid to see the world for what it has become

Normally, I would give you a big old "no shit" and keep on with my day. Instead, I decided to read the comments which, to my delight, is filled with alt-right people

1) making mind-numbingly philistine readings of the article that, despite all their purple prose, fails to grasp the most basic arguments of the piece.

2) whining incessently about the decadence of modern SCPs, insisting that people who don't agree with them are too stupid to see the community for what it has become.

You, honorable gentlebeast, have earned the SCP Medal of Distinction in the field of trolling the alt-right.

Your post was so meta that it spilled out of the actual article and all over the comments page.

Addendum 1-J:

You did so well at personifying the alt right that you might want to take this article down before Jeff Sessions tries to arrest you for being a jew lover being unpatriotic

Addendum 1-Actual:

Seriously, good work, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They are the self-propagation of the cancer that the ghost fucked into Diana, and then birthed into me.

When I saw this line on your author's page, I knew it's gonna be something interesting.

It's a very strange one though. I can't really tell if I'm enjoying the concept of an SCP trying to destroy humanity with internet memes, but it surely doesn't feel like it's out of place. With today's mass culture something like this had to appear, so I can see where this came from. One thing I particularly liked is that destruction of humanity via memes never feels like it will be an inevitable end of the world scenario. The article wants you to guess. It is obvious that the meme machine will believe that its methods will lead to humanity's downfall, but everything can turn out differently. The fact that this threat is so blurry fits well. So me will upvote.

UPD: So I saw this post in one social media today, it was an "E" meme. It had 169 likes. I guess machine is right, we are doomed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who gave you the idea this was supposed to be subtle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just about as deep and subtle as the actual skip itself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like yelling a slur, then responding with "if you're offended then maybe the slur has some truth to it XD".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My balls. My fat fucking nuts.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very in-depth commentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

"we live in a society": the scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What? You shot the man breaking into your house? Shame on you."

Then it was never really going to be for you, because that's part of the point. That's the way the computer is, because that's how her creator was.

It seems as though my original comment was not well-constructed, and ended up being misunderstood as a result. For that, I apologize. Here's a better way of saying this:

The concept of an SCP destroying the world using memes is extremely interesting, and can be fleshed out into a great skip, and also a great commentary about today's flaws, especially with meme culture and how fast lies can spread. Although, it seems to be more built to say "racist memes are bad and people who are racist have mental issues" than to really dig into hidden gold.

Also, being a user with the knowledge of the controversy occurring at the time this skip was written, it seems more like a stab at those you don't like (albeit for good reason, though still merely a cheap shot) than a fleshed out skip. Perhaps new users in the future, long after this controversy has blown over, may enjoy it slightly more. Although, it will most likely be not extremely memorable to them. And yet, it could have been.

That's my critique. I apologize for not properly taking the time to explain my thoughts in length.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There were a few things I didn't like, mainly in terms of phrasing, things I felt could have been described more clinically but I enjoyed reading this, it is well written and invokes a heightened emotional response plus a little mystery without going as far to shove the whole thing down my throat while waving it's hands around going "what does it all mean?!? whoooooo"
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EU more PU amirite

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This conversation isn't relevant to the article at all, but the EU has not banned memes. The law in question hasn't actually been voted on yet, just passed to a vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
O O F. The EU has now banned memes. Looks like they'll have to get their memes from the black market.

Imagine if people in the EU go to buy memes from meme dealers, but they get scammed into buying shit memes instead. I fucking love how the EU banning memes is a meme in itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Block
- Block

After some thought, I have decided to downvote it. The more that I look at the article and think about its themes, the more that I hate it. I've read almost a thousand SCPs and tales now… and I think this might be one of the worst of them.

When I talk about its imagery or Rose's goal being ridiculous or mention "propaganda", what I've been trying to get across to you is that you crossed the line. You took what could have been a well executed revenge (suicide?) story or a slow burn horror SCP or a joke SCP and instead made a faux tragedy.

Firstly, The nameless guy comes across to me as a caricature of an alt-right supporter. Specifically, he's a soulless effigy that invites the reader to bring a pitchfork and torch. Caricatures of this severity are glaring red flags to me. While they can work if done correctly, I believe that you failed because the majority of his journal entries can be removed while still keeping the plot intact. All the journal entries appear to add is content that is meant to offend alt-right supporters and incite anger in the rest (Why else does Entry 417 exist?). I still believe this even after hearing that you wrote this piece a year ago. I despise the alt-right, but writing about a member ejaculating on a photo and rape… that went too far in my book.

Secondly, despite being a powerful AI and clearly antagonistic to the nameless guy, Rose is somehow unable to deviate from her programming and more or less does what he wants her to do. Her paragraph near the end about already being dead may explain her actions, but it's flimsy. Diana died from gunfire after she and the nameless guy shot at the police. That's not really a smart way to go, and it's absurd for Rose to hold a grudge against all of humanity for it. I am instead lead to believe that Rose is merely following orders. To put it another way: She does what the plot demands because the plot demands it.

Thirdly, her plan is just dumb. Even given current events there's no way that I could possibly believe that non-anomalous deepfried memes could literally end the world. That plan in and of itself is a caricature of a stereotypical Neck Beard's plan for world devastation. Other readers believe that this plan could work, and their views are alien to me. Personally, I feel that this plan might make for a good joke or a slow burn horror piece, except that it contains an alt-right manifesto, rape implications, and Rose's necessarily long exposition.

Fourthly, with regards to the plot, I don't understand Diana's purpose. I feel that you could cut her out of the story, have the nameless guy create Rose, and the only thing that would be lost is the shred of humanity that Diana and Rose possessed. I don't feel that those shreds matter a whole lot because this piece is extremely cruel in nature with or without Diana.

It's not a good drama or horror piece because it isn't believable and it isn't a good comedic piece because you took it too seriously. Worst of all, the entire thing is dehumanizing. Both Rose and the nameless guy lack humanity, and as such the philosophical points that are present here have an artificial quality to them. Therefore, this SCP is a faux tragedy.

I must apologize if my critique here is too incendiary, but it felt necessary to be a bit more crass than usual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sadly, those are fairly accurate depictions of posts I've actually seen while reading things that are bad for my blood pressure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean I could've gone down this guys way of thinking. Thinking others as lesser because they think something different then you? It's fucking pathetic and I'm glad I hadn't gone down that route.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The journal entries feel incredibly strawman-y and narmy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw this sitting in your sandbox a LONG time ago while thinking "I wonder when he'll post this".

Good to see it up. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was something I remember, but maybe it's for the best not to add to Mghost's rapsheet when he's despicable enough on his own. I like your idea re: giving Diana a voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how they posted it with strikethrough, presumably in order to make it ambiguous if they were serious or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean I don't disagree with the person in the article.

That is a strange claim to make my friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah this is definitely not a new article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing I can say about this (due to the comment continuing to come up) is that I've definitely read this in draft form about a year ago, if not more. There's records of it existing around that time on his sandbox then, if one digs through revision histories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhh, I'm not sure. It's definitely well written, but it feels a bit on the nose. Particularly the diary entries of the ghost, though if that was intended to be an over-the-top parody, and as such fit the theme that sometimes terrible things will be laughed at and memed, I'd give it a pass.

Yet still, can't help but feel this in itself is kind of a reactionary response to recent events to stick it to those "homophobes" who don't like the current management or logo. Maybe I'm wrong, but the timing is a bit suspicious. On top of that, this kind of just feels like SCP-3078, but a bit better written with politics.

No vote for now.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: Please no. Just like violence is never the answer to violence, bad article isn't the answer to bad article. Writers' time is better spent making themselves write better article than bashing each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean I don't disagree with the person in the article. He's just a bit, er, pathetic. Not rational. Not willing to find a nonviolent solution.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
This is so bad, maybe we should start decommissioning articles again

Sure, pal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, I thought the diaries were overdone, but then I remembered reading enough news to know there's a very good chance there might be people like that, straight to the ejaculation. Also, I won't exclude the idea that memes will destroy us all.

Since this SCP supposedly takes place around 2010, shouldn't the memes be about rage comics with derpina and trollface?
Oh right, this is some deep statement about American meme culture and politics that will be looked back fondly in 20 years as classic of 2018
But maybe it's just the dumb yuro speaking in me that does not get the subtlety of The Great American Meme Machine :P
This is so bad, maybe we should start decommissioning articles again
I rate this pool'sclosed/+9000 desu
*dabs*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an excellent piece, because it's just ludicrous enough to make you think "this would never work"… but then you stop, read back a bit and think "…but haven't things like this already happened, sorta?" It's not realistic, but it's close enough to realistic to make you wonder. +1 and I wish I had another to give
